name: Trivrit
scientificName: Operculina turpethum (L.) Silva Manso (Syn. Ipomoea turpethum)
usesInAyurveda: Tridoshic Modulation: Powerful Pitta and Kapha Hara (pacifies Pitta and Kapha doshas). Due to its Tikta (bitter) and Katu (pungent) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Virechana' (therapeutic purgation) and 'Bhedana' (systemic downward-moving) therapies. It clears the fiery and heavy qualities of Pitta and Kapha through its systemic purging property. It may increase Vata if used in excess.
Karma (Actions): Virechana (purgative), Shothahara (anti-swelling), Jwarahara (antipyretic), Krimighna (anthelminthic), and Vishaghna (anti-toxic).

API/AFI Adherence: The dried root/stem bark (specifically the root bark) is official as per the Ayurvedic Pharmacopoeia of India (API). It is the central ingredient in almost all classical purgative formulations like Avipattikara Churna, Trivrit Lehyam, and Abhayarishta.
Specific Indications: Used in Udavarta (bloating/constipation), Kamala (Jaundice), Shotha (Edema), Arsha (Hemorrhoids), Kushtha (Skin diseases), and Jwara (fever).
usageForm: Root bark powder (Churna), Medicated jam (Lehya), Fermented extract (Arishta), Medicated ghee (Ghrita).
mainMedicinalUses: World-Class Purgation Specialist (Virechana): Trivrit is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for therapeutic purgation (Virechana). Its 'Virechana' (purgative) and 'Tikshna' (sharp) nature provided by turpethin glycosides helps in expelling deep-seated Pitta and metabolic toxins (Ama) from the lower gastrointestinal tract. WHO-aligned research recognizes its potent laxative and anti-inflammatory properties.

Liver and Jaundice Care: Known as 'Kamala-hara'. It effectively clears 'Pitta-Avarana' (Pitta-obstruction) in the liver by promoting smooth evacuation of bile and systemic toxins through its 'Sara' (flowing) quality. It is a key herb for managing recovery from hepatitis and jaundice.

Skin Disease and Itch Relief: Its 'Virechana' property membantu dalam sistemik detoxifying of the blood, making it invaluable for chronic skin conditions like psoriasis and eczema. It is traditionally used to improve skin radiance and reduce 'Kshuda-pidika' (acne).

Inflammation and Edema (Shotha): Systemically used for clearing localized swelling (Shotha) and abdominal enlargement (Udara-roga) by expelling accumulated fluids and metabolic wastes through the gut.

Worm and Internal Parasite Care: Its extremely bitter-pungent nature makes it incomparable for managing stubborn intestinal parasites and worms (Krimighna) that thrive in a high-Kapha environment. It helps in cleared stagnant toxins from the gut.

Phytochemical Profile: Rich in Glycosides (Turpethin, Turpethinic acid), Flavonoids, Steroids, and diversos Pentacyclic Triterpenes. Turpethin is the primary bioactive responsible for its potent purgative and anti-inflammatory action.
plantProfile: Unique History: Classified under the 'Virechana' (purgative) group in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. The name 'Trivrit' literally translates to 'three-fold' (referring to its triangular stems). It is spiritually associated with 'Sattva' (purity). Mentioned in classical Sanskrit literature as the 'Indian Jalap'.

Physical Characteristics: A perennial, climbing or trailing herb reaching up to 5-10 meters in height, with quadrangular-to-triangular winged stems and opposite, heart-shaped (cordate), leathery leaves. The flowers are attractive, funnel-shaped, and usually white-to-pale pink. Found throughout India and tropical regions worldwide in deciduous forests and wastelands of the sub-Himalayan tract and South India.

Specific Chemical Compounds: Contains Turpethosides, Jalapinic acid, and Sitosterol. The presence of turpethin (approx. 5-10% in root bark) is crucial for its neuro-relaxant, anti-tumor, and anti-tubercular action on the heart and skin cells.

Strict Standardizations: The root bark is standardized in API based on its yellowish-brown-to-grey color and characteristic estremamente bitter-pungent taste. Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<8%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>15%). HPLC monitors turpethin levels.
preparationMethods: Root Bark Powder (Churna): 1-3g of fine powder taken with warm water at bedtime for systemic detoxification and laxation.
Trivrit Lehyam: Medicated jam-like preparation taken (10-20g) after meals for managing chronic gut infections and piles through its blood-purifying action.
Abhayarishta: 15-30ml of fermented extract taken twice daily after meals for managing chronic gut infections and piles through its 'Sara' quality.
Avipattikara Churna: Combined with other digestive and cooling herbs for systemic acidity and constipation relief. Take 3-6g with water before meals.
External Paste (Lepa): Ground fresh root or powder applied directly to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, skin rashes, hyperpigmentation) to reduce heat and swelling.

dosages.Children: Generally NOT recommended internally in large doses. Small quantities (125-250mg) under professional guidance.
dosages.Adults: Powder: 1-3 grams; Lehya: 10-20 g; Arishta: 15-30 ml, divided throughout the day before meals.
dosages.Elderly: Very low doses (500mg - 1g) are advisable initially, monitoring for any increase in dizziness or intestinal cramping.

safetyProfile: Contraindications: Strictly contraindicated in individuals with extreme high Vata (severe constellation, dehydration), during pregnancy (abortifacient risk), and during active extreme diarrhea. Toxicity is low, but purgative effects can be severe.
GRAS Status: Classified as a safe medicinal herb with a history of use spanning millennia across various global traditional systems without documented toxicity.
Herb-Drug Interactions: Monitor if taken alongside medications as it significantly increases the bioavailability of many drugs.
Adverse Effects: Overdose results in excessive watery stools, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalance. Discontinue if extreme purgation results in nausea.
